slides.title: You Know the Fundamentals!
slides.body: Variables, loops, functions, lists, dictionaries — you have learned **the core of Python**. Almost every real-world Python program is built from exactly these pieces.
slides.tip: Finishing a course is rare. You should feel proud.

slides.title: Building Beats Reading
slides.body: The fastest way to improve is to **build something real**, even if it is messy. Every bug you fight teaches you more than any tutorial.
slides.code:
import random

secret = random.randint(1, 100)
guess = int(input("Guess: "))
print("Correct!" if guess == secret else "Wrong!")
slides.tip: You already know enough to build a guessing game — try it.

slides.title: Track What You Learn
slides.body: A simple **learning log** keeps you accountable and shows how far you've come. Write one line a day — it adds up fast.
slides.code:
with open("learning_log.txt", "a") as log:
    entry = input("What did you learn today? ")
    log.write(entry + "\n")
print("Logged! Keep going.")
slides.output:
What did you learn today? I built the number guessing game!
Logged! Keep going.
slides.tip: Six lines of code, a habit that lasts a lifetime.

slides.title: Read Code, Join a Community
slides.body: Browse **GitHub** to read real projects in topics you care about. Join **r/learnpython** or **Python Discord** — every beginner question is welcome there.
slides.tip: Reading other people's code is like reading novels: the more you read, the better your own writing becomes.

slides.title: Pick One Project — Start Today
slides.body: Don't wait until you feel "ready" — that feeling never comes on its own. **Start small, finish it**, then build something slightly bigger. That is exactly how every professional did it.
slides.tip: 30 minutes a day beats a 4-hour session on Saturday. Consistency wins.
